user_config.goal_statement: Be known as the team that ships cloud + AI tools, not just talks about them. Target: 3 inbound enquiries per month from CTOs at mid-size companies.

assets.name: CloudCorrect
assets.one_liner: Open-source AWS audit tool with 65 checks across 12 services

assets.name: DocProof
assets.one_liner: AI-powered document verification tool for enterprise

posts.content: Shipped CloudCorrect v2. 65 AWS checks across 12 services. MIT licensed. If your cloud bill surprises you, this is where to start.
CloudCorrect v2 setup walkthrough with architecture diagrams and sample output for r/aws
A client processed 400 documents through DocProof in a single day. That's 3 days of manual work eliminated.
Thinking about why most AI tools promise transformation but deliver incremental improvement. The gap is execution, not capability.
5 lessons from building AI tools for enterprises that actually get used. Thread.
The future of cloud auditing is automated, continuous, and open-source. Here's why we bet on this direction.
Hot take: most cloud audit tools are designed for compliance teams, not engineers. We built CloudCorrect for engineers first.

common_edits: Removed 'excited to share' opening, replaced with direct statement
Added specific client metric that was in the brain dump but not in the draft

previous_monologues.monologue: Week 1. Strong start. CloudCorrect launch hit the right audience — 4 CTO connection requests from LinkedIn alone. Reddit post also performed well. The proof-of-execution angle is landing. Need to get DocProof into the pipeline next week with a similar outcome-first approach.

Week 2. DocProof post was decent but didn't generate the same CTO-level engagement as CloudCorrect. The '400 documents' stat is strong but the framing was too product-focused. The 'AI tools promise vs deliver' insight post got high impressions but zero CTO connections — it attracted the wrong audience. Concerning pattern: we're drifting toward thought leadership. Need to pull back to proof-of-execution.
